subject: Chemistry
title: How Sunscreen Protects Skin
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on How Sunscreen Protects Skin.

hook.text: Sunscreen contains molecules that absorb ultraviolet light and convert it harmlessly into heat.

buildup.text: UV rays from the Sun damage DNA in skin cells. This damage can cause sunburn and skin cancer.

discovery.text: Chemical sunscreens like avobenzone absorb UV photons. Their electrons get excited, then release energy as heat.

twist.text: Mineral sunscreens (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) work differently—they scatter and reflect UV like tiny mirrors.

climax.text: SPF 30 blocks 97% of UVB rays. SPF 50 blocks 98%. The difference is smaller than most people think.

punchline.text: Absorb UV, release heat. Molecules that take the bullet for your DNA.

quiz.question: How do chemical sunscreens protect your skin?
options: They absorb UV photons and release the energy as harmless heat
They reflect all sunlight away from the skin
They thicken the outer layer of skin
